items.title: The Ostrich Effect
items.description: If you’re looking for a reason to despair over the state of American education, there are many places you could start. You could, for example, worry about the all-consuming phones—or the gambling and TikTok addictions that come with them. You could worry about the free fall in literacy , the decline in math fluency, the rise of AI, the insanity of college-obsessed parents, the sharp increase in truancy, the teen mental-health crisis, and on and on. Yet something that deserves more attention is damage to free expression—not in the classroom but in the public sphere of high school: the realms of student newspapers, student government, and other aspects of everyday life. School is meant to prepare students for the real world, but too many institutions are training kids to be silent. The three of us are the editors of our high-school student newspaper in Washington, D.C., with a staff of about 60 student journalists. We ask a lot of questions. We want to know when and why things happened. We cover big policy changes (a ban on phones) and small schedule changes (an earlier start date to the school year). We cover all kinds of controversies—among them, senior pranks, changes in faculty, and debates about assemblies and theatrical productions. Writers argue in our opinion section about calculator theft, the cure for teenage sleep deprivation, grade inflation, AI policies, and more. [ Christopher L. Eisgruber: The myth of the campus snowflake ] Although many teachers at our school agree to interviews, some consistently decline them. They tell us they’re afraid that saying the wrong thing will result in a stern lecture from a boss or come up in a future job interview. They say they do not want their views at a specific moment in time to be permanently findable on the internet. That’s their right, of course. And we could use more people in society who know that they don’t have to opine on everything just because they can. But we ask teachers for interviews because they are experts who often have more information about the inner workings of the school than we do. We want our publication to be like a small-town newspaper, one that gives its readers a comprehensive view of how our community thinks and feels, and one that advances the cause of seeking the truth and reporting it. But our readers cannot get a full view of the community when people are too afraid to talk. And people are definitely afraid. This problem, as we see it, is much bigger than our school newspaper. The era of social media came with the promise of hyper-democratization—no longer do you need to be an established pundit to get your message out. (All of us, born in the 2000s, struggle to imagine it any other way.) Although there is much to celebrate about the ways in which the internet has connected us, the permanence of the internet has given rise to censorship pressures—both individually and institutionally. People are so nervous about saying the wrong thing that they choose to say nothing. Taken to the extreme, a culture of silence can be extraordinarily harmful. As a society, we have to accept that people’s opinions change over time. We can start by treating high school as a time for students to take intellectual risks. In this phase of life, students discover their interests and form their opinions. They try out for sports teams and join clubs and meet new people. And through that experimentation, they come into themselves and grow more independent. The adults who care about students—parents, teachers, school administrators, prospective employers—should encourage them to speak up, to debate, and, yes, even to fail. And they should do so without stepping in to intervene at every turn. Recently, the Montgomery County, Maryland, school district made local headlines after officials required administrators to review student newspapers prior to publication and outright censor certain reports based on vague standards. School officials tend to approach such matters with varying degrees of subtlety: One administrator at our school floated the idea of a prior-review system for our articles and instructed us on how we should contact sources, while another told us our decision to publish an article about them was reprehensible. Administrators owe it to their students to support a culture of free expression, and high schoolers need to keep contributing to that culture by saying what they think. Free expression is a principle that seems easy to uphold in theory, but requires some degree of courage in practice. To start, we must reaffirm that our individual and collective right to free expression transcends the substance of anything that any one of us might say. A robust—and sometimes messy—marketplace of ideas is in the best interest of all of us. [ Read: In praise of ‘difficult’ kids ] In Des Moines, Iowa, in 1965, five students wore black armbands emblazoned with peace symbols to school in protest of the U.S.’s escalating involvement in the Vietnam War. Local high-school administrators suspended three of the five students. The school district soon found itself justifying the suspensions in a three-year legal battle that ended with a Supreme Court victory for the students in Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District . The Des Moines administrators said they blocked the armbands because of the possibility that the silent protest could create a disruption, even though no disruption occurred. If a police officer were to prohibit a person on the street from wearing a black armband because that person could hypothetically incite a large protest, any reasonable American would rightly see the officer’s actions as a violation of the First Amendment. The same logic should apply across society. Of course, creating a culture of openness and respectful debate requires more than staving off government interference. If large groups of people decide that silence is safer than risking disagreement, and if they instruct children to be silent in turn, our fundamental right to free expression will crumble from within.

items.title: The Other Victims of a Wrongful Conviction
items.description: Photographs by Desiree Rios “I bet you’re extremely happy ,” Jay Young texted me. “So you and the slimy media going to fawn over his side and forget about my dad’s family and friends now?” It was March 10, 2021, and Benjamine Spencer, the man serving a life sentence for murdering Young’s father, Jeffrey, was about to be freed. The state of Texas had concluded that 34 years before, it had almost surely convicted an innocent man; a Dallas judge was expected to rule in Spencer’s favor any day. Young held me partly responsible for this turn of events. My reporting for The Atlantic had unearthed exculpatory evidence that helped bring about Spencer’s release. I texted back, offering to talk. He demurred. For three years, we barely spoke. Then, in August, 2024, a few days after the publication of my book Bringing Ben Home , Young sent me a congratulatory message. “I have zero ill will or negative feelings toward you or anything or anyone,” he wrote. “I would really appreciate a conversation.” I’d rather not , I thought. Young distrusted the media. Years earlier, he had reluctantly granted me an interview for my Atlantic story. Our interactions since then had been prickly; I didn’t relish starting a new round. [ From the January/February 2018 issue: Can you prove your innocence without DNA? ] But something about his words—the psychological distance between his two texts—snagged my attention. What had happened in those three-plus years to turn his anger into warmth? Over time, from dozens of conversations with Spencer, I had developed a fairly detailed map of the inmate’s legal, emotional, and spiritual journey. But Jay Young? I knew almost nothing about him. Every time an innocent person is convicted and later exonerated for a violent crime—something that has occurred more than 2,800 times in the past four decades in America—there are two sets of victims, each suffering a unique torture. The innocent prisoner loses years of freedom, while his children, spouse, parents, siblings, and friends are left with a jagged hole in their lives. But on the other side, the people devastated by a violent crime—the victim (if he survives), his family and friends—also suffer years of chaos as the exoneration plays out. In many cases, no one is brought to account in the end. When an innocent man is freed, the stories of those victims tend to go overlooked. In this regard, I had failed Jay and the other members of Jeffrey Young’s family. I would try to fix that. Over the next 18 months, Jay Young and I spoke repeatedly, and I came to see that the legal system had thrown him, too, into a kind of prison. He had watched helplessly as the case against his father’s alleged killer had crumbled and Spencer walked out of prison to thunderous applause. I had not registered Young’s solitary, emotionally treacherous journey—one marked by suicidality, loneliness, and addiction. If I had, I would have discovered that Ben Spencer and Jay Young, two men on opposite sides of a murder, had both been betrayed by the system that was supposed to protect them. Jeffrey Young was working late at his office in a warehouse district of Dallas on March 22, 1987, a Sunday night. The acting president of a clothing-import company, he was white, 33 years old, married to his high-school sweetheart, and the father of three preteens. There were no security cameras to capture what happened that night, but police later theorized that two men waited in the empty parking lot. When Young walked out of his office, they tackled him, stole his cash and jewelry, struck him on the head, and cracked his skull in five places. They placed Young in the back seat or trunk of his BMW and drove over the Trinity River into West Dallas, a poor Black neighborhood reeling from the relentless violence of the crack epidemic. Somehow, Young managed to escape the car, falling out onto the street, where he lay bleeding and shaking as a crowd gathered. Spooked, the perpetrators abandoned the BMW in a nearby alley and ran away. Young died at Parkland Memorial Hospital at 3:05 a.m. The randomness of Young’s murder terrified white Dallas, and it spurred Ross Perot, a billionaire and soon-to-be presidential candidate, to offer a reward through his company: $25,000 for evidence leading to the arrest and indictment of Jeffrey Young’s killers—a sizable sum in 1987. Four days after the assault, police detained Benjamine Spencer. He was Black, 22 years old, newly married with a baby on the way. He had no record of violence. Jay Young, who was 12 years old when his father was killed, recalls greeting the news with ambivalence. “I felt like, I trust law enforcement and they found the right person. But I remember thinking: That’s great, but it’s not going to bring my dad back ,” he told me in 2017. Over the seven months after the murder, police conducted an investigation that, with the clarity of hindsight, resembled a checklist of how to convict an innocent man. No physical evidence connected Spencer to the crime. The evidence consisted of the testimony of three neighbors who sought to claim Perot’s $25,000 reward, and of a jailhouse informant who sought a more lenient sentence. Facing up to 25 years, the informant walked out of prison two months after his testimony. Finally, police ignored a far more plausible suspect. At trial, the prosecutor said nothing as at least two of his four witnesses lied under oath, and he hid evidence that would have discredited the state’s star witness. The resulting guilty verdict was thrown out, but after a second trial, Spencer was convicted and sentenced to life in prison on March 28, 1988. The murder robbed Jay Young of his hero and the family of its financial and emotional stability. Greg Ward, who was Young’s best friend in ninth grade, recalls a home in perpetual gloom, the family burdened by “sadness, confusion, desperation.” Young could usually bury his pain: He was handsome and popular, always had a girlfriend, and played varsity sports. “But he had fits of rage and anger,” Ward told me. Desiree Rios for The Atlantic Jeffrey Young was killed as he left his office in Dallas. The police and prosecutors cannot be blamed for the first tragedy—that fault lies with the murderers alone—but the second tragedy was their doing. The damage to Spencer, and to the Youngs, would not surface until the case against Spencer began to unravel. T hirteen years passed . On May 20, 2001, Jim McCloskey sat in the visitors’ room at Coffield Unit, a maximum-security prison 100 miles south of Dallas. McCloskey’s organization, Centurion Ministries, was the first national organization to reinvestigate dubious convictions, and it had a near-perfect win record. He told Spencer they were taking his case. McCloskey quickly found problems with the conviction: the monetary incentive for the eyewitnesses, the informant’s lies, the police failure to investigate a more viable suspect, the optical impossibility of identifying anyone running from the car on that dark night. He offered the findings to The Dallas Morning News , which ran stories and editorials detailing the flaws. Suddenly, Young recalled, his father’s murder was everywhere—the regular subject of media coverage, court proceedings, and public protests. “I said, Really? Is this going to be the rest of my life? ” Prosecutors blocked Spencer’s path at every turn. When McCloskey and Cheryl Wattley, Spencer’s new attorney, asked the district attorney’s office to look at their evidence, the DA declined. When they filed a writ of habeas corpus with a Dallas judge, requesting a hearing to consider McCloskey’s new evidence, the prosecutors fought it. They assured the Young family that legal challenges and media coverage were typical (they were not) and told them not to worry, because the conviction was “ironclad.” For the Youngs, every article, every court filing, every parole hearing dragged them back to the day their father died. Determined not to let the prisoner they held responsible go free, they waged their own campaign to keep Spencer in prison. Doing so was exhausting. “I don’t think that a lot of people actually understand the thought process and the emotions that you have to go through,” Jimmy Young, Jay’s younger brother, told me in 2017—fielding calls from journalists, organizing letter campaigns against Spencer’s parole, meeting to strategize their arguments before the parole board. “We have to go to parole hearings every year. Every year . And the reason why we all do that is because we believe in the justice system” and, he added, “we believe that you should pay for what you did.” Inside Coffield, Spencer was paying for something he did not do. But innocent prisoners, who deserve to be angry, can’t afford the luxury. Spencer believed that bitterness would destroy his soul. It was a “cancer,” he said, and he trained his mind to eradicate the smallest traces. He prayed for the neighbors who framed him. He clung to the hope that one day he would be cleared. Most of all, he thought about the Young family. “My heart truly goes out to them, for they have suffered the greatest loss,” he wrote his then-ex-wife, Debra. (He and Debra remarried in 2022.) Later, when the family opposed his appeals and his parole, he wrote, “I understand their position … They trusted the authorities to find and prosecute the person(s) responsible.” Over the objections of the district attorney, Dallas County District Judge Rick Magnis held an evidentiary hearing in July 2007. Jay Young, then 32, sat in the packed courtroom behind the state’s appellate lawyers. The hearing was a “circus,” he fumed in 2020, recalling that day, and “Magnis was the ringleader.” The judge took over the questioning at times, grilling the eyewitnesses and the lead homicide detective, openly skeptical of their testimony. “It was a joke in my opinion.” But as Young watched the proceedings, doubt stole into his mind, he told me nearly a decade later. The lawyers for the DA’s office acted “lackadaisical,” he said, texting on their phones and failing to rebut Spencer’s arguments or rehabilitate their own discredited witnesses. “I remember sitting there going, Something’s not right ,” Young recently told me. “But I just never really wanted to open those wounds.” Judge Magnis found that Spencer deserved a new trial based on “actual innocence,” and recommended that the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals allow a new trial to proceed. But the district attorney opposed the judge’s finding and repeatedly assured the Youngs that the decision would be overturned. In 2010, while both sides waited for the court to weigh in, prosecutors reinterviewed the state’s star witness, Gladys Oliver. She told them that she had received $5,000 to $10,000 in reward money in exchange for her testimony. She had almost certainly lied about it at trial, which was grounds for vacating the conviction. But instead of turning over the damning evidence to Spencer’s attorneys or the court, the prosecutors placed the interview notes in a file cabinet and closed the drawer. Two prosecutors later told me they had, at the time, believed that the interview wasn’t relevant. This defies belief: Evidence undermining the credibility of the state’s most convincing witness could have swayed the judges still considering Spencer’s case. But the judges never saw the notes. In 2011, the Court of Criminal Appeals rejected Magnis’s finding and ruled unanimously that Spencer would serve out his life sentence. The decision devastated Spencer, who had believed he was about to be freed. “I don’t want you to worry about me, but I’m really struggling right now,” he wrote Debra shortly after the ruling. “I keep wondering: Is prison the life that my future holds for me?” But he quickly rebounded. “I knew that I didn’t commit this offense and God knew the truth about the offense, and in that I had freedom,” he later explained to me. “I’ve always been free, even though I was incarcerated.” Desiree Rios for The Atlantic Jay Young displays a photograph of his father, Jeffrey Young. Jay Young felt no victory, only dread. “I just remember thinking, This isn’t over ,” he told me in 2017. It was the latest move in a game that would continue with parole hearings, newspaper stories, rallies, and the prospect that the next elected district attorney might open a new investigation. Spencer’s case had become the cacophonous soundtrack of his life. In those years, Young faced his darkest period. His career derailed. His marriage ended in 2014. He had custody of his two children half the time; the other half, alone in his home, he began to drink too much. It’s impossible to calculate how much of Young’s trouble sprung from the trauma of losing his dad, and how much arose from the disabling chaos that erupted every time his father’s murder resurfaced in the courts and the media. “He was lost and he was in trouble,” his friend Greg Ward remembered. “Jay is one of the smartest people I know. Couldn’t keep a job. Couldn’t keep a family. Couldn’t keep things running because he was always running himself.” “I was in a prison of depression and anger,” Young reflected when we talked earlier this year. “It kind of took my life.” Spencer, incarcerated for life, felt free. Young, who could come and go as he pleased, was shackled to a trauma that never seemed to end. Y oung’s prediction that the case would resurface came true in 2017, when I arrived in Dallas on assignment for The Atlantic . Along with Daryl Parker, a private investigator who believed that Spencer was innocent, I spent weeks canvassing down-and-out parts of Dallas, trying to dig up evidence 30 years after the crime. We found one “eyewitness” who admitted that he had accused Spencer for the reward money; later, he signed an affidavit and passed a polygraph stating he had lied at trial. We found the jailhouse informant, who casually told us that he had made up Spencer’s confession to get a shorter sentence. We found a new alibi witness who insisted that Spencer had been at his home at the time of the assault. Because the second “eyewitness” was dead, the conviction now rested entirely on the testimony of the star witness, Gladys Oliver, who refused to talk with us. The Atlantic article ran in the winter of 2018. Soon thereafter, Spencer’s defense team asked John Creuzot, the newly elected district attorney, to reopen the case. Cruezot’s prosecutors confirmed our evidence and found more: the notes from the 2010 interview with Gladys Oliver, tucked away in a file cabinet, showing that she, too, had perjured herself in court. “We’ve had it all along,” Creuzot told me. “We just weren’t honest about it.” In January 2021, the DA’s office joined Spencer’s attorneys in court to argue that Spencer had not received a fair trial and should be released while the state decided whether to retry him or dismiss the charges. The judge agreed, and on March 12, 2021, Ben Spencer walked out of the Dallas jail. Outside, a crowd of supporters had swelled to more than 200 and, against security protocol, the guards opened the doors to the lobby of the jail. Supporters surged in, laughing, singing, high-fiving. Spencer and Debra walked from the jail into the lobby and halted, dumbstruck, as people started cheering. They inched forward, holding hands, smiling. The sea of people parted as they walked out of the building into the warm March evening. Jay Young learned that Spencer was free hours later, when he received a text from a reporter seeking a comment. In the coverage, Jeffrey Young was barely mentioned, and then only to set up Spencer’s triumph over the system. Jay Young was justified when he sent me his text: So you and the slimy media going to fawn over his side and forget about my dad’s family and friends now? “T hat person is dead ,” Young said, when I brought up the text earlier this year. “I’m a different person.” Around the time he sent me that message five years ago, Young had stopped drinking and was wrestling with emotions that he had for years anesthetized with alcohol. “I was slowly falling down into the pit,” he later told me about that time. Over the next three years, he threw himself into a 12-step program and his church as if his life depended on them—which it did. On May 15, 2024, the Court of Criminal Appeals ruled that Spencer had not received a fair trial. This allowed District Attorney Creuzot to decide whether to exonerate him. Over the summer, Creuzot and his lead prosecutor, Cynthia Garza, met once with several members of the Young family and once with Jay alone. They told Young that they planned to exonerate Spencer, but they did not explain their reasoning. “It’s like my dad died again,” Young reflected afterward, in one of the many conversations we have had this year. “I felt very disrespected, like they didn’t care and somehow [it was] my fault.” Young had called Creuzot a “coward,” bringing the meeting to an unhappy end. Desiree Rios for The Atlantic Debra Spencer It struck me as unfair to take these feelings out on the current DA. Creuzot and his prosecutors did not convict an innocent person; they were correcting their predecessors’ mistakes. Young reached out to Jennifer Thompson—a woman who famously identified an innocent man as her rapist, and who personally understands the damage to victims on both sides of a wrongful conviction—for guidance. In her view, the Young family had every reason to be angry. The system had failed them in 1987, and it tried to cover its mistakes for three decades. The new prosecutor kept the Youngs in the dark until the last minute and presented Spencer’s exoneration as a fait accompli. Doing so “re-traumatized them,” Thompson told me. “You’re leaving them with all the questions and none of the answers.” [ Elizabeth Bruenig: The one area where a 14-year-old is an adult ] Thompson, who founded Healing Justice , an organization dedicated to helping victims of violence and people who were wrongly convicted navigate toward reconciliation, said that anyone in the Young family’s position would struggle to know who or what to believe. Dallas prosecutors told them one thing for 30 years, Thompson said, “and then the system is like, Just kidding! ” On August 29, 2024, more than 150 of Spencer’s supporters, double the room’s listed capacity, packed into the 283rd District Court in Dallas. Thirty-seven years ago, Spencer had been convicted for murder in this very court. Now he would learn whether he would be absolved. Wedged into a crowded bench, I heard my phone ping. “I’m here,” the text said, and standing at the door was Jay Young, looking shell-shocked as he scanned the room. The crowd hushed as Garza, the prosecutor, announced that Spencer should be exonerated based on actual innocence. Soon Spencer—tall, lanky, and elegant in his blue pinstripe suit—stood up; he was visibly nervous. Unaware that the victim’s son sat 30 feet away, he spoke for less than two minutes, chiefly to point out that he and his family were not the only ones who had been harmed by the system. “The Young family were victims as well, because they lost a loved one, and the person who was actually responsible for what happened to Jeffrey Young was not brought to justice,” he said. “So I’d ask that you pray for the Young family and be mindful of them. I don’t want them to be forgotten.” Young stared at his feet, betraying no emotion. Afterward, in the hallway, I asked Young, “What do you think?” “I think my body would have told me if I was in the presence of my dad’s murderer,” he murmured. “It didn’t.” L et’s survey the landscape from Jay Young’s perspective as it looked on that morning in August 2024. His father’s murder was now a 37-year-old cold case. There was no DNA, the witnesses were scattered or dead, and barring a confession from the killer, his murder would most likely never be solved. Any “closure”—if there is such a thing—had evaporated. Ripping off a Band-Aid didn’t begin to capture the pain, Young said. “It was like ripping a freaking scar that’s already been healed.” He understands the importance of making things right for Spencer, who can never recover those years. When the detectives took him in for questioning, he was 22, newly married and starting a family; he walked out a 56-year-old man with gray hair and a grown son. The Youngs’ loss, not to mention the emotional upheaval they experienced because of three decades of prosecutorial intransigence, was to the outside world a footnote in Spencer’s story. “We just felt forgotten,” Young said. “Ben is a victim. But we lost a life.” Young recognized, for perhaps the first time, that the judicial system is a zero-sum game. For years, Spencer was the loser, but one day in August 2024 the prosecutor flipped a switch, and the Youngs were left with less than nothing. Young was forced to reframe his entire story. The man he had hated for decades and whose parole his family had thwarted year after year was now deemed faultless. “Everything changed in my life all at once,” Young said. “And I was tired of fighting.” Three weeks after Spencer’s exoneration, Young was alone in his house in Frisco, just north of Dallas, ruminating on his life. He found it unbearably bleak: 50 years old, divorced, isolated from his family, his career off track. His girlfriend was gone, and so was the one constant in his life: his belief that his dad’s killer had been caught and punished. “I remember hearing these voices in my head saying, Just go end your life. And I said, You know what? Okay. ” He walked toward the closet in his bedroom, where he stored three guns. He saw his reflection in the mirror. What am I doing? he thought. He called his mentor in his 12-step program. “I need someone to come over here and take my guns,” he said. He was going for a walk; he’d leave the front door unlocked. It was noon and the neighborhood streets were empty when Young, emotionally spent, collapsed on the sidewalk. “I remember how hot it was because I took my shirt off and I can feel the concrete. I remember looking up, and I said, ‘God, I cannot do this anymore. I cannot do this.’” He made his way home, checked that the guns were gone, and fell into an exhausted sleep early that night. He awakened confused by an unfamiliar emotion: peace. The interior clamor had stilled. “I cannot explain it. That’s how it feels to wake up the next day after surrendering.” He marvels that none of his concerns—his separation from his girlfriend, unhappiness with his work, worry over his finances—had evaporated. This peace was untethered to his circumstances. “I was just happy.” Young serves as a greeter at Hope Fellowship, a Texas megachurch with more than 7,000 attendees across four campuses. Over coffee one day in early 2025, Young told Dustin Chappell, one of the pastors, that when he was a child, his father had been murdered—and the man convicted for it had recently been exonerated. Chappell was floored: He had just met a man in church who had also been exonerated. Some weeks later, Chappell sat down with Young and Charles Chatman, who had spent 27 years in prison for rape before DNA evidence cleared him. When Young began talking about Ben Spencer, Chappell later recalled, “Charles happens to mention, ‘Yeah, I know Ben.’ And Jay’s like, ‘Wait, what?’ And Charles said, ‘Yeah, I was in prison with Ben.’” Chatman did not know Spencer well at Coffield Unit, but he knew that Spencer’s behavior was not the behavior of a guilty man. He didn’t hustle, didn’t smuggle in cigarettes or other contraband and sell them at a premium. “Those [innocent] guys have a special way, a different way of thinking,” Chatman told me. After Chatman was exonerated in 2008, he visited Spencer in the Dallas jail where he was staying during his evidentiary hearing. “I ask him, ‘Man to man, face to face: Did you do that crime?’ When he told me, face to face, ‘Charles, man, I did not do this crime,’ looking in his eyes, I decided then that I would be behind him.” Seventeen years later, at a Starbucks near the church, Young asked Chatman if he thought Spencer was innocent, and Chatman said that he did. “Jay’s whole countenance changes,” Chappell recalled. “He says to Charles, ‘You know, I’d really like to meet Ben.’” When I heard this story, I thought about the only time I had seen Spencer near tears. Here’s a man who had spent three decades in a maximum-security prison in Texas, who had survived gang wars and lockdowns and 100-degree heat with no air-conditioning. I never heard him complain. One day in June 2021, three months after Spencer’s release, we were eating sandwiches on a shady bench outside the Hall Arts Hotel, where he worked as a facilities engineer. I asked him what he most wanted in life, now that he was free. “I know that my greatest wish may never come true, but I continue to pray and hope for it.” He cleared his throat quietly, and I could see that he was choking back tears. “My wish is that one day the Young family could just come to accept the truth—the fact that I’m innocent.” And I thought: That will never happen. It happened this way . Soon after his conversation with Young, Chatman told Spencer that Young had asked to meet with him. He expected Spencer to demur—why would he want to unearth his painful past? But Spencer told him he’d drive up to Hope Fellowship the first Sunday he could. On March 2, 2025, in the church’s overflow room, Spencer met Chatman, who texted Young, and a few minutes later, Young walked out of the sanctuary. “Jay looked at me and looked at Spencer, just with a big smile on his face,” he told me. “Then he put his hand out to shake hands, and it was like slow motion,” Young recalled, picking up the story when we talked a few days later. “That was when it locked it up 100 percent. Once I shook his hand, that’s when everything made sense. Like, you read a book and you don’t know what’s going to happen. Why is this happening? And then you get to the last chapter and you just go: I get it .” As all three later recounted to me, they spoke for an hour and then decided to find a restaurant for lunch. Nervously at first, but then more intimately, they talked about their families—Spencer’s newly reunited family and Young’s newly divided one. They talked about the Bible. Young described his struggle with alcohol and depression. “It was weird because it was like I had known this guy forever,” Young told me. “Because I have.” Finally, Young broached the question that mattered most. “I have to ask this because I want to hear it from your mouth and not from newspapers or other people. But me asking you, this isn’t accusing you. I just want to hear you say you didn’t do it.’ “I didn’t,” Spencer promised. “Can you forgive me for going to those parole hearings?” Young asked. “Man, you only were going by what you were told,” Spencer said, laughing. “I would have done the same thing.” The Christian Church, Pastor Chappell reflected, has spent 2,000 years preaching about grace and forgiveness, but to see people live out those qualities—well, that takes his breath away. “In a world that is full of chaos and hatred and polarization , to see unity where the world says unity shouldn’t exist, I think is nothing short of beautiful,” he said in wonder. Put aside the Christian language for a moment. My sense, having watched the ticktock of this unlikely reconciliation, is that the story transcends religion. It speaks to a uniquely human capacity, for two people to lift up their eyes from their grievances to find a more expansive landscape, to pursue an uncomfortable truth even when it robs them of family solidarity and the sweet taste of vindictiveness, to understand “the enemy” and recognize he is nothing of the sort. “I had freedom that I’d never felt before,” Young told me recently. “In my life. About anything.” That afternoon, I received a text from Spencer: “Guess who I’m having lunch with?” In the photo, Spencer is wearing a tan suit with a plaid tie and a tie clip. Young is wearing a Team Hope sweatshirt and blue jeans. Both men are beaming. At home that night, Young was studying photos from their lunch: Spencer’s broad smile, his arm around Young’s shoulders, his body relaxed. “I could tell how happy he was,” Young said. “And I thought: He gets to start his life over. And I get to start my life over too .” F ive months later , Spencer’s friends and family packed into a restaurant in downtown Dallas to celebrate Spencer’s one-year anniversary as an exonerated man. People key to Spencer’s exoneration—Judge Rick Magnis, Spencer’s two lawyers, and Daryl Parker, the investigator who worked with me to find exculpatory evidence—chatted quietly in the corner until the toasts began. Spencer glanced toward the door, then suddenly bounded across the room and lunged at a man, almost knocking him over in a hug. “This is Jay Young, Jeffrey Young’s son,” Spencer announced. The room hushed. After a beat, everyone stood and began to clap until the place pounded with emotion. Judge Magnis strode over and shook Young’s hand: “Really happy for you, and proud.” Cheryl Wattley, Spencer’s longtime attorney, did the same. Young looked from the judge and to the attorney, his onetime nemeses. “Good work. Good for y’all,” he said. But it was Debra and Ben’s friends and family who moved him. They stood in line to thank him, tongue-tied; some were crying. [ Barbara Bradley Hagerty: What I learned helping free an innocent man ] The next morning, Young sent Debra a text. “I felt like I was with family/friends. You guys are inspiring. Love you guys.” Debra responded: “I can’t tell you how many people have asked about you already this morning. We love you!” Desiree Rios for The Atlantic Benjamine Spencer and Jay Young You can’t choose your family, but you can choose your tribe. True, tribes can be anchored around DNA, ethnicity, religion, or politics. But they can also cohere around a set of shared values: here, the search for truth and the hunger for freedom, whether physical or emotional. Jay Young and Ben Spencer chose each other. “We’re not supposed to be friends,” Young reflected recently, laughing. “You probably have better odds of winning the lottery than this happening.” How is it that Young and Spencer can sit side by side, so different in fundamental ways: white and Black; raised middle-class and poor; a staunch Republican and a newly registered Democrat; a white-collar businessman and an ex-con; one blamed for the murder of the other’s father? I don’t claim to be an expert on reconciliation, but allow me to tell you one essential element I observed as their story unfolded: choice . Reconciliation grows out of a series of quiet choices. Spencer and Young were each trying to find a way out of his own subterranean labyrinth, and at every juncture, he had to choose which way to turn. The choices were counterintuitive, incremental, and always hard. Spencer chose to set aside bitterness, to forgive his enemies, to keep the Youngs’ pain at the forefront of his thought; he refused to break prison rules or hustle for cash or join a gang for his protection. And once he was out, he drove up to Frisco and met one of his main adversaries, a man who helped keep him in prison for an extra 14 years. Young also encountered a series of difficult choices: to listen to his doubts, to put down his final drink, to choose spiritual surrender over suicide, to witness Spencer’s exoneration in a sea of supporters, to meet the man who he once believed had killed his father, and to celebrate that man’s freedom. The two adversaries, whose lives were derailed by a flawed system, have emerged victorious—and friends.

items.title: Selling Beyond Charisma: Your best salesperson is your biggest business risk
items.description: The most charismatic person on your sales team is quietly making your company impossible to scale — and most leaders are rewarding them for it. There is a particular kind of salesperson every Ghanaian business celebrates. The one who walks into a bank’s procurement office and is greeted by name. The one whose phone the client answers at 9 pm. The one who closes the deal nobody else could. We promote them, we protect them, and we build our revenue around them. And that is precisely the problem. When a company’s revenue depends on personality, it has not built a sales capability. It has built a dependency. The uncomfortable truth most boards avoid is this: your most charismatic salesperson may be your single largest point of failure. The day they resign — or get poached by a competitor who knows exactly what they are worth — your pipeline walks out the door with them. “When revenue depends on personality, you haven’t built a capability. You’ve built a dependency.” I have watched this happen across Accra and beyond. A family-owned distributor whose entire client book lived in one man’s head. An insurance firm where three “star” agents wrote most of the new business, and the founder slept badly every month they were unhappy. A growing IT services company that could not understand why doubling its headcount did not double its revenue — because the new hires had charm but no system to plug into. This is the cost of relationship-led selling without structure. Relationships matter in African business; they always will. But a relationship that lives only in one person’s memory is not an asset on your balance sheet. It is institutional risk wearing a good suit. “A relationship that lives only in one person’s memory isn’t an asset on your balance sheet. It’s institutional risk wearing a good suit.” The Rainmaker Trap Call it the Rainmaker Trap: the better your star performs, the less anyone else needs to learn — so the more dependent you become on the very person you cannot afford to lose. Charisma masks the absence of a system. Everything looks healthy until the rainmaker leaves, and then you discover you never had a sales organisation. You had one talented individual and a great deal of hope. A real sales system makes performance repeatable. It defines how leads are generated, qualified, followed up, converted and retained. It documents how objections are handled and why deals are lost. It trains people to diagnose a customer’s problem, not merely present a product. And it survives turnover, because the knowledge lives in the company, not the individual. “Charisma masks the absence of a system. Everything looks healthy — until the rainmaker leaves.” What to do this week Run the Resignation Test. Pick your top performer and ask one question: if they resigned this morning, how much of our pipeline could anyone else continue? The honest answer tells you how much risk you are carrying. Document one deal, end to end. Take a single live opportunity and write down every step — how it was sourced, what the objections were, what moved it forward. You have just turned instinct into a repeatable play. Change one coaching conversation. In your next review, stop asking “what’s your number?” and start asking “show me how you handled the silence after the proposal.” Coach the behaviour, not only the target market, because the sale is most often lost in that silence, not in the pitch. Standardise your follow-up. Pick the one stage where deals quietly die, usually the follow-up, and make it a defined, expected discipline rather than a personal habit. What this means for the organisation For business, this is not a sales-team issue. It is a valuation issue. Buyers, investors and partners pay for revenue that is systematic and defensible — not revenue that is hostage to one person’s mood or mobile number. A company that sells through discipline can grow beyond the reach of its strongest individual. A company that sells through charisma grows only as far as that individual can personally carry it, and shrinks the moment they walk. Charisma should be an advantage your system amplifies — not the operating model your company depends on. T he question worth arguing about So here is the one to put on the table at your next leadership meeting: if your best salesperson resigned this morning, how much of your revenue would walk out the door with them — and who, honestly, could replace them? If that number makes you uncomfortable, that discomfort is the work. At MGA Consulting Ghana Limited, we help leadership teams convert personality-dependent selling into a documented, coachable sales system — starting with a Sales Dependency Audit that maps exactly where your revenue lives in people versus process. Book one at michaelabbiw.com and find out how much of your sales engine would survive a resignation letter.

items.title: La Steg autorise le stockage par batterie pour le solaire résidentiel
items.description: La Société tunisienne de l’électricité et du gaz (Steg) a officiellement autorisé l’installation de systèmes de stockage par batterie pour l’énergie produite par des installations photovoltaïques résidentielles raccordées au réseau national basse tension. Cette mesure concerne les systèmes équipés d’onduleurs hybrides capables de gérer simultanément la production solaire, le stockage d’énergie et le raccordement au réseau public. Cette décision permettra aux ménages équipés de panneaux solaires de stocker l’électricité produite durant les heures d’ensoleillement maximal et de l’utiliser ultérieurement, y compris lors de coupures de courant temporaires liées aux opérations d’équilibrage de la charge du réseau. Cette initiative intervient alors que la Tunisie connaît des températures exceptionnellement élevées et une forte hausse de la demande en électricité ; ces dernières semaines, cette situation a exercé une pression considérable sur le réseau, entraînant des délestages programmés ou des coupures de courant dans diverses régions du pays. La Steg a publié de nouvelles spécifications techniques définissant les exigences minimales pour la conception, l’installation et la maintenance des systèmes photovoltaïques avec stockage par batterie. Ce document s’adresse aux utilisateurs finaux, aux services techniques de la compagnie d’électricité et aux installateurs agréés. Selon l’entreprise publique, le respect de ces exigences est essentiel pour protéger les installations domestiques et garantir la sécurité du personnel de maintenance du réseau, en empêchant les systèmes privés d’injecter de l’électricité dans les lignes lors des travaux d’entretien. Le groupe «Énergies renouvelables» de la Confédération nationale des entreprises citoyennes de Tunisie (Conect) a salué la publication de ces spécifications, la qualifiant d’étape importante dans le développement du cadre technique et réglementaire du secteur. L’organisation a souligné que l’intégration de l’énergie solaire, du stockage et du réseau devrait contribuer à accroître l’autoconsommation, à renforcer la sécurité de l’approvisionnement et à accélérer la transition énergétique dans le pays. Tunisie | Le stockage d’électricité à domicile pour parer aux pannes de réseau L’article La Steg autorise le stockage par batterie pour le solaire résidentiel est apparu en premier sur Kapitalis .

items.title: Mid-Year Budget: No new taxes expected – Dr Theo Acheampong
items.description: Technical Advisor at the Ministry of Finance, Dr Theophilius Acheampong, says the 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review will focus on the government’s fiscal performance, expenditure, borrowing and key policy initiatives, with no plans to introduce new taxes. Speaking on the Super Morning Show on Thursday, July 23, ahead of the minister’s presentation to Parliament, Dr Acheampong said the review would provide a comprehensive assessment of the economy nearly eight months after the 2026 budget was presented. “The minister is going to Parliament this afternoon to give an update on where we are—what we’re doing on revenue, what we’re doing on expenditure, what we’re doing on borrowing, what we’re doing on the special initiatives, and whether there is any need for supplementary estimates,” he said. According to him, the review is expected to focus on the government’s fiscal performance and any adjustments needed to keep public finances on course, rather than announcing major policy shifts. Dr Acheampong stressed that the government is not considering new tax measures, arguing that Ghana’s fiscal challenge lies more in expenditure management than revenue mobilisation. “I have argued, even before going into government, that we have a major expenditure problem, less so a revenue problem,” he stated. “You can raise more revenue, but if it keeps going into a leaking basket, you’ve got a problem. The issue is tightening expenditure, ensuring that you’re spending well and spending in the right places.” Instead of introducing fresh taxes, he said the government intends to improve tax administration by strengthening compliance among existing taxpayers. “It is not a question of more taxes,” he emphasised. “Rather, it is about enforcing compliance and ensuring that those who are supposed to pay taxes actually do so.” READ ALSO: Finance Minister to present 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review today Dr Acheampong further disclosed that the government is undertaking a comprehensive review of key tax legislation, including the Income Tax Act, customs laws and excise duties, to make the country’s tax system more responsive. “We’ve taken a fundamental review of the core tax laws. Some measures may be announced in the Mid-Year Budget Review, while others will take more time to implement,” he said. Beyond fiscal policy, he indicated that the finance minister will also provide updates on major infrastructure and agricultural projects, including road construction, agro-commerce initiatives and other interventions aimed at improving food transportation and boosting economic activity. “You will get an update on some of the key projects and interventions. The minister will outline where government is and what progress has been made,” he added. Finance Minister Dr Cassiel Ato Forson is expected to present the 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review to Parliament, providing an update on the economy’s performance in the first half of the fiscal year, the implementation of the 2026 Budget and the government’s revised fiscal and economic outlook for the rest of the year.

items.title: Barca sign Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund for €29m
items.description: Barcelona signed forward Karim Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund on Thursday for a reported fee of 29 million euros ($33 million) including potential add-ons. “Adeyemi becomes a new Barca player for the next five seasons, until June 30, 2031,” said Barcelona in a statement. The versatile 24-year-old is Barca’s second signing after they splashed out on England international Anthony Gordon from Newcastle. Lightning quick, Adeyemi, who can play up front or on either flank, bolsters Barca’s attacking options after the departure of veteran striker Robert Lewandowski. Barcelona also lost Marcus Rashford after his loan from Manchester United ended, leaving them short in attack. The Spanish champions have also made a bid for Atletico Madrid forward Julian Alvarez, who is their primary target this summer. Barca coach Hansi Flick gave Adeyemi his debut for the German national team in 2021. The attacker joined Dortmund in 2022 from Red Bull Salzburg, netting 36 goals in 146 games across all competitions during four seasons before his Barca switch. AFP The post Barca sign Adeyemi from Borussia Dortmund for €29m appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Access Bank Ghana wins two prestigious honours at Euromoney Awards for Excellence
items.description: Access Bank Ghana Plc has been named Best Bank for Consumer Lending and Best Bank for SMEs at the prestigious Euromoney Awards for Excellence 2026. This reaffirms the Bank’s commitment to helping individuals, families and businesses achieve their ambitions through accessible, innovative and customer-centred financial solutions. The dual recognition forms part of 16 awards won by the Access Bank Group across its African operations, underscoring the Group’s continued leadership in delivering meaningful impact for customers across the continent. For more than three decades, the Euromoney Awards for Excellence have been regarded as one of the banking industry’s most respected benchmarks, recognising institutions that demonstrate excellence in leadership, innovation, governance, customer outcomes, strategic execution and long-term value creation. For Access Bank Ghana, these awards are ultimately a recognition of the customers it serves every day. Whether helping a young professional purchase their first vehicle, supporting a family through an important life milestone, or providing an entrepreneur with the capital and expertise needed to grow a business, the Bank continues to develop solutions that respond to the real aspirations behind every banking need. This customer-first approach reflects the Bank’s belief that banking is about far more than financial products; it is about enabling people to thrive. Over the past year, Access Bank Ghana has continued to expand access to finance through initiatives such as its Pick Now Pay Later, Pay Day Loan, Retail Personal Loan, Fly Now Pay Later, Mortgage Facility, Auto Loan, and a range of tailored SME financing solutions designed to make credit more accessible while supporting enterprise growth across Ghana. Recognising that access to finance alone is not enough, the Bank has also strengthened its SME ecosystem through strategic partnerships with organisations including the International Finance Corporation (IFC), DHL Group, Deloitte Ghana and Birmingham City University. These collaborations provide businesses with access to financing, business advisory services, trade opportunities, capacity-building programmes and international networks, enabling entrepreneurs to build stronger and more competitive businesses. Speaking on the achievement, Board Chair of Access Bank (Ghana) Plc, Ama Bawuah, said, “These awards reflect our unwavering commitment to the customers and businesses we exist to serve. As a Board, we have remained focused on building a strong, well-governed institution that creates sustainable value for all our stakeholders. “We are proud that this recognition affirms not only our strategic direction but also our commitment to supporting Ghanaian families, entrepreneurs and businesses as they pursue their ambitions and contribute to national development.” Commenting on the recognition, Managing Director of Access Bank (Ghana) Plc, Pearl Nkrumah, said the awards represent far more than institutional achievement. “Everything we do begins with our customers. Every solution we design, every product we introduce, and every innovation we pursue is driven by one purpose: helping our customers thrive. “Being recognised as Best Bank for Consumer Lending and Best Bank for SMEs is a powerful validation of that commitment and reflects our determination to empower individuals, support businesses, and create opportunities that contribute to Ghana’s economic growth. We are honoured by this recognition from Euromoney and deeply grateful to our customers for their trust, our partners for their collaboration, and our dedicated colleagues, whose passion, commitment, and unwavering focus on our customers make achievements like this possible.” The Group’s 2026 Euromoney successes span customer experience, SME banking, sustainable finance, digital banking, corporate responsibility and market leadership across several African markets. For Access Bank Ghana, these awards are not simply a recognition of past achievements; they reinforce the Bank’s commitment to continuing to innovate and deliver solutions that respond to the evolving needs of customers. As the Bank continues to grow, its focus remains unchanged: understanding what matters most to its customers and providing the support they need to realise their ambitions.

items.title: No more referee-six tests for Ghana’s economic reset
items.description: In November 2025, this column asked whether Ghana’s 2026 Budget would live up to its promised “reset,” and concluded that the intentions were sound, but execution would be the true test. Eight months on, the scorecard reads better than most predicted. The Head of Research at First National Bank, Julien Ayippey, asserts that the cedi has held broadly stable. He explained further that this has been made possible as the IMF-supported programme has concluded successfully. The domestic bond market has reopened after a three-year absence. Public debt has stayed contained, and the Sinking Fund, whose absence we flagged last year, is now operational, funded by 7% of gross non-oil tax revenue and foreign-currency dividends. “Holding the line on spending amid real infrastructure, social and political pressure is itself an achievement worth recognising.” He mentioned that sceptics will call this restraint rather than transformation. That debate is legitimate, but discipline must endure the political cycle, and better headline numbers must translate into better jobs and living standards. Today, the finance minister, Dr. Cassiel Ato Forson presents the Mid-Year Budget Review to Parliament. Julien themes out some critical questions on whether Ghana can sustain stability without the IMF as referee and whether households facing rising transport, rent and school-fee costs will feel it. He describes six issues that should frame this mid-year review. The inflation Turn Inflation fell from 23.5% in January 2025 to 3.2% in March 2026, a low last seen in 1985, then reversed for three straight months to 5.3% in June, driven by transport, rents and school fees. Non-food items now make up 68.5% of the basket’s inflation, with services rising 9.4%. Renewed U.S.–Iran tension in the Strait of Hormuz has pushed Brent crude back above US$89 after easing to US$84 in June, and Ghana imports almost all its refined fuel. The Bank of Ghana has held its policy rate at 14% since May. June’s 5.3% still sits inside the Budget’s 8% end-year target, but the direction and pace deserve a straight answer, “What is the current assumption for fuel prices, and what shields the Budget if the conflict persists”? An open forecast revision beats a quiet one. Credibility depends on candour here. Show the plan for the 2027–28 debt wall Ghana faces roughly GH¢50.3 billion in domestic debt maturities in 2027 and GH¢45.8 billion in 2028, alongside US$1 billion in Eurobond repayments in 2026 and US$2 billion in 2027, fixed dates that will not move. The good news: it has rarely been cheaper to prepare. The 91-day Treasury bill has fallen from 28.4% in January 2025 to about 5.9% today, and April’s post-default seven-year bond raised GH¢2.7 billion at 12.5%. The plan to raise GH¢20.2 billion in seven-to-ten-year paper by year-end is the right instinct, a functioning yield curve is also a pricing benchmark for corporate paper, and First National Bank stands ready to support issuers and investors accessing it. What we need on today is disclosure on: — The current balance in the revived Sinking Fund, and the specific flows feeding it. — Progress against the GH¢20.2 billion target at the halfway mark. — A quarterly bond issuance calendar, requested since November and still overdue. Recent T-bill auctions have undershot, and yields are drifting up weekly. A published calendar remains the cheapest confidence-building tool the Ministry has. Name what replaces the IMF’s discipline Completing the Extended Credit Facility is a genuine milestone, and the shift to the Fund’s Policy Coordination Instrument, monitoring without money, is a sensible bridge. Ghana’s fiscal discipline however, has been historically held under supervision and slipped without it, and an election cycle is now the real test. The review should confirm the primary balance at half-year against the 1.5%-of-GDP target (1.2% through March), and whether revenue is tracking the GH¢268 billion goal. The single most important promise in this Budget is zero central bank financing in 2026; a clean half-year confirmation would outweigh any speech. Turn cheap money into jobs, not just cheap borrowing Lower rates are already moving the banking system. Private-sector credit grew 41.2% year-on-year to about GH¢119.6 billion by June, 34% in real terms, while non-performing loans fell from 23.1% to 16.1%. With the policy rate down from 28% to 14%, conditions for a genuine lending cycle exist for the first time in five years. But credit growth is not living standards. The review should throw light on the utilisation of the GH¢10 billion Big Push infrastructure bond and job numbers from the 24-hour economy programme, delivery, not plans. “Savers financed this crisis. The review owes them a plan, not a footnote.” There is a cost to this rotation that deserves attention: a saver who placed GH¢10,000 in a 91-day bill now earns about GH¢146 over three months, against roughly GH¢600 two years ago, a near-zero real return at 5.3% inflation. Longer-dated bonds, collective investment schemes and the Ghana Stock Exchange all offer a better path for that capital, and the Budget should point to it explicitly. Prove the cocoa reset can pay farmers on time For 32 years, an offshore syndicated loan financed each cocoa season, pledging 70–92% of the harvest to foreign lenders and costing over US$150 million in interest on the 2023/24 facility alone. Its 2024/25 replacement, upfront payment by global buyers, left farmers waiting when traders held back. From 2026/27, COCOBOD will instead fund the crop domestically in cedis through a roughly US$1 billion bond and commercial-paper programme, open to pension funds and non-resident investors. A companion Cocoa Board Bill will bar quasi-fiscal spending, guarantee farmers at least 70% of the gross export price, and mandate that half the crop be processed locally, changes that matter to roughly 800,000 farming households. The timing is unforgiving: the international cocoa price has more than halved, from about US$9,155 a tonne in June 2025 to US$4,272 in June 2026, with Ghana’s realised price down 52% year-to-date to US$3,748, squeezing revenue that must also service COCOBOD’s existing GH¢32 billion debt. “I’m hopeful that there will be a clear confirmation of the issuance timetable and whether farmer arrears from the failed 2024/25 season are fully cleared. We also need to explain how the domestic market absorbs cocoa bonds alongside the GH¢20.2 billion sovereign programme and the GH¢10 billion Big Push bonds without pushing yields back up”, Julien added. Level with us on gold and the cedi The cedi’s recovery from about GH¢16 to the dollar in October 2024 to GH¢11.45 by May 2026 is real and visible in the price of appliances, medicine and cars. It is also being tested: the rate closed at GH¢11.55 on 17 July, a 9.5% year-to-date depreciation. Record gold prices did much of the work behind the recovery, but gold’s monthly average has fallen from above US$5,000 an ounce in February to about US$4,240 in June as markets price a possible Fed rate move in September. Gross reserves fell from US$14.2 billion in March to US$12.9 billion in June, a US$1.2 billion drawdown in a single quarter, taking import cover from 5.7 to 5.0 months. “A windfall saved is a buffer; a windfall spent is next year’s crisis. The questions are what gold and cocoa prices does the Budget now assume and how much of the reserve build is being treated as cyclical windfall rather than permanent income? What is the contingency if gold falls a further 15%?”, Julien asks. The bard for today’s review Government has earned a measure of trust: inflation brought under control, the bond market reopened, the IMF programme completed, the currency stabilised. But the second half is harder, inflation is creeping back, the Gulf remains volatile, IMF oversight is winding down, and the 2027 debt wall is eighteen months away. On 23 July, Ghana does not need celebration. It needs hard numbers, an honest forecast, and delivery figures on the programmes that touch daily life. “This review is government’s chance to show its promised reset can hold without a safety net beneath it”, he concludes.

items.title: « Ma Première École, Ma Maison… » : « Nous devons, nous-mêmes, incarner et porter les valeurs que nous voulons voir dans notre société » (Christelle Kalhoulé, Children Believe)
items.description: Dans la lancée de sa campagne : « Ma Première École : l'éducation commence à la maison, la protection commence dans la famille », qu'elle met en œuvre en collaboration avec ses partenaires, l'ONG Children Believe a initié un forum des leaders coutumiers et religieux, le mercredi 22 juillet 2026 à Ouagadougou. L'objectif général est de mobiliser ces leaders communautaires autour de la promotion d'une parentalité engagée et protectrice, en les positionnant comme ambassadeurs du changement de normes sociales dans leurs communautés respectives. Cette assise visait donc, de façon spécifique, à analyser les réalités actuelles de l'exercice du rôle parental et des responsabilités familiales dans notre pays, en identifiant les principaux défis et les opportunités pour le bien-être et le développement de l'enfant ; partager les données probantes sur les bénéfices de l'engagement parental pour les enfants, les familles et les communautés. Il s'agit également de permettre à ces leaders communautaires d'exprimer leurs perceptions, préoccupations et résistances sur le sujet de la parentalité engagée ; explorer les ancrages culturels, traditionnels et religieux qui soutiennent le rôle protecteur et éducatif des parents, notamment les pères. Par ce cadre, Children Believe entend, en outre, adopter collectivement des engagements concrets de plaidoyer et de sensibilisation au sein des communautés de ces leaders religieux et coutumiers ainsi qu'identifier des messages-clés adaptés à chaque communauté confessionnelle et coutumière. Ici, la cérémonie d'ouverture, présidée par la directrice régionale de Children Believe-Afrique de l'Ouest, Christelle Kalhoulé, avec à ses côtés, des leaders religieux et coutumier. En effet, la famille est considérée comme la cellule de base de toute société humaine et, partant, le cadre idéal d'éducation et de formation des enfants. Elle implique donc, pour les pères et mères, un rôle prépondérant dans le suivi-contrôle de l'évolution au quotidien des enfants dans le cercle familial et au niveau communautaire. Cependant, le constat est à ce jour établi que la famille s'est métamorphosée, avec de nouvelles configurations, et fait face à de nombreuses difficultés favorisées par la perte de valeurs socio-culturelles et de bien d'autres facteurs. Cette situation se traduit, entre autres, par des dérives sociales et des attitudes qui mettent à mal toute la société, sapent la stabilité nationale et la cohésion sociale (non-respect des lois, du bien public et privé, défiance à l'autorité de l'État, non-respect des valeurs humaines, etc.). D'où le sens de l'initiative « Ma Première École, Ma Maison : l'éducation commence à la maison, la protection commence dans la famille » de Children Believe . Cette campagne, mise en œuvre depuis janvier 2026, s'articule autour d'actions de communication, de plaidoyer, de mobilisation et d'événementiel, alliant approches traditionnelles et digitales, déployées de manière progressive pour toucher les familles, les communautés et les décideurs. Depuis son lancement, elle a, à son actif donc, plusieurs actions de communication et de plaidoyer, des panels d'échanges, une conférence publique, etc. Ces activités ont ainsi permis de mobiliser de nombreux et divers acteurs sur cette question essentielle de la place de la famille, particulièrement celle du père, dans l'éducation et la protection de l'enfant. « Tout commence déjà dans la famille » La cérémonie d'ouverture, présidée par la directrice régionale de Children Believe-Afrique de l'Ouest, Christelle Kalhoulé, a situé l'environnement global du sujet. Pour mettre en relief l'enjeu du sujet, Mme la directrice régionale a comparé la petite enfance à un jardin, avec ceci en commun que les deux ont besoin d'être ‘'préparés'', entretenus pour pouvoir produire les résultats attendus. « Malheureusement, au fil du temps (et aussi par les contingences du développement), l'éducation de nos enfants a été complètement reléguée aux enseignants. Alors que dans la réalité, le père et la mère sont les premiers éducateurs des enfants. Les enfants ont besoin d'être guidés, non seulement par des valeurs, des connaissances, et tout commence déjà dans la famille. Nous avons pensé que cette initiative, que nous avons portée à notre manière, n'est pas l'apanage d'un ministère, c'est une préoccupation de toute la société », a posé la directrice régionale de Children Believe-Afrique de l'Ouest, saluant au passage les efforts du gouvernement en faveur de la famille. Elle rappelle que l'enfant ne grandit pas seulement grâce à l'école et tout commence à partir de la famille. C'est pourquoi elle invite également à l'éducation par les actes, par l'exemple. « C'est dire que nous devons, nous-mêmes, incarner et porter les valeurs que nous voulons voir dans notre société. Et les enfants, généralement, n'écoutent pas seulement (j'allais dire n'écoutent pas) ce que nous disons, mais regardent comment nous vivons, comment nous nous comportons, comment nous parlons aux autres, comment nous traitons les autres… C'est le premier espace d'apprentissage pour l'enfant », interpelle Christelle Kalhoulé, regrettant que la famille, censée être un espace de protection, d'éducation, ne le soit parfois pas. De son avis, il faut chercher des solutions, afin de pouvoir construire le Burkinabè, l'Africain de demain que l'on souhaite. Cela est d'autant fondamental que, justifie-t-elle, « la plus grande richesse d'une nation, ce sont ses hommes, c'est-à-dire le capital humain, qui se construit à partir de la petite enfance ». Préserver la famille de la perte de ses valeurs primordiales Le représentant des partenaires, l'abbé Paulin Damiba, de l'OCADES Caritas Koupèla, note que le thème est d'un grand intérêt pour toute société humaine. « La tradition de l'Église a toujours vu la famille comme une institution divine, unie, organisée et ordonnée à une vie d'amour à l'instar de la Sainte Trinité. Dieu père, fils et Saint-Esprit », a-t-il souligné avant d'ajouter que Dieu a voulu que la famille soit à la genèse du monde, créant ainsi Adam et Ève. L'abbé Paulin Damiba. Le prêtre a également rappelé que la famille et l'éducation ont toujours été au cœur de la pastorale de l'Église. « Donc, ce thème est d'une grande importance, parce que nous voyons qu'au fil du temps, la famille est vraiment en train de perdre ses valeurs primordiales », justifie-t-il donc la pertinence de l'initiative. Selon le directeur des programmes de Children Believe, Kassoum Morgo, ces acteurs jouent un rôle très important dans la transformation sociale. On y retient qu'ils côtoient au quotidien les familles à l'occasion des prières, des mariages, des naissances, des baptêmes ou des funérailles. Ils détiennent une autorité morale et une légitimité pour impulser, au sein de leurs communautés respectives, un changement durable des normes sociales en faveur d'une parentalité plus engagée et plus protectrice. « Ce sont eux qui contribuent à véhiculer les normes et les valeurs sociales, et qui permettent aux familles également de pouvoir vivre en harmonie », soutient M. Morgo, lançant un appel aux parents à plus de responsabilité au niveau de la famille. « Les enfants doivent avoir les modèles et ce sont les parents qui peuvent être les premiers visages que l'enfant peut regarder pour pouvoir prendre pour exemples. Nous lançons également un appel aux acteurs qui sont invités ici, les leaders coutumiers et religieux, qui contribuent à façonner nos sociétés, que les citoyens de demain que nous recherchons doivent commencer d'abord par le modèle que nous inculquons aux enfants dans les familles », commente le directeur des programmes, qui exhorte de ce fait chacun à plus d'implication dans l'éducation, pour une meilleure société de demain. Le directeur des programmes, Kassoum Morgo. La cérémonie d'ouverture a été aussi marquée par le témoignage d'un père modèle, Joseph Kaboré, de la communauté de Tanghin, dans la province du Ganzourgou. Ce dernier a d'abord salué l'impact de l'initiative de Children Believe, notamment en termes d'éveil des consciences. « On ne peut pas mettre au monde un enfant et le laisser aux mains de la société. Nous avons compris qu'un enfant, c'est comme un jardin, il a besoin d'être arrosé pour donner les résultats que nous attendons. Il faut surveiller les faits et gestes des enfants, les guider, les écouter, leur montrer ce qui est bon, ce qui n'est pas bon… Par exemple, lorsque je vois un enfant s'amuser avec un jouet en pistolet, je le retire et le remplace par un jouet en voiture », s'est confié M. Kaboré, en langue nationale. Ce père modèle insiste également sur le fait que le tout ne se limite pas à répondre aux besoins matériels des enfants, il faut leur consacrer du temps, leur apprendre des valeurs en passant par des jeux avec eux, etc. Cette assise a donc permis aux acteurs participants de procéder à un état des lieux de l'engagement parental au Burkina Faso et de partager les expériences de pères modèles issus des communautés d'intervention. Le père modèle, Joseph Kaboré, a félicité l'ONG Children Believe et ses partenaires pour la pertinence de l'initiative pour la société burkinabè. La journée a été également marquée par les interventions des leaders communautaires pour partager leurs expériences et témoigner de leur engagement aux côtés de Children Believe. Aussi, chaque communauté religieuse et coutumière a présenté les réalités de l'engagement des parents dans son contexte, le tout suivi d'échanges et de débats en plénière autour de ces réalités exposées par les communautés. Le forum s'est achevé par l'adoption de messages et engagements communs. Lire sur le même sujet : Burkina/Protection de l'enfance : Children Believe fait des médias des alliés de l'éducation familiale O.L. Lefaso.net

items.title: Automobile : l’allemand MAN reprend le montage en Algérie
items.description: Après plusieurs années d'arrêt, les camions et bus du constructeur allemand MAN sont de nouveau assemblés en Algérie . Le constructeur allemand des véhicules industriels annonce la reprise de la production sur son site industriel de Blida en collaboration avec MTC (Maghreb Truck Company), son partenaire local historique depuis 2003. Reprise de la production de camions à l’usine MAN en Algérie Les deux partenaires ont maintenu l’usine de Blida même pendant la période d'arrêt de la production. L'usine dispose d'une capacité de production allant jusqu'à huit véhicules par jour et offre une grande flexibilité grâce à ses deux lignes d'assemblage parallèles, indiqué MAN Trucks & Bus sur son site internet officiel. Selon la demande du marché, les deux lignes peuvent produire des châssis de camions et d'autobus. Actuellement, l'accent est mis sur les modèles de la gamme MAN TGS en configurations 4×2, 6×4 et 8×4, principalement destinés au transport longue distance, précise la même source. MAN : un objectif de 1.500 véhicules par an pour le marché algérien En Algérie, l'objectif de MAN qui fait partie du géant automobile Volkswagen, est de livrer environ 1.500 véhicules par an sur le marché algérien à moyen terme, en s'appuyant sur “les solides bases déjà établies pour assurer une croissance future”, ajoute le constructeur allemand. La reprise de la production en Algérie s'accompagne d'investissements ciblés dans les infrastructures, les procédés et la formation. Le groupe a dispensé des formations techniques à ses employés locaux afin de garantir le respect des normes de qualité internationales et de renforcer l'expertise locale en matière de production. “En combinant savoir-faire local et expertise internationale, MAN met en place une base de production performante, conçue pour une croissance durable sur le marché nord-africain”, assure le constructeur. MTC emploie en Algérie environ 250 personnes, dont une équipe de 50 personnes travaillant dans la production de CKD sur le site de Blida, ce qui constitue “une base solide pour la reprise des opérations d'assemblage local”. L’entreprise exploite en outre des centres de services de « haute qualité » à Oran, Sétif et Laghouat, “assurant un soutien à la clientèle à l'échelle nationale et renforçant la position de leader de MAN sur le marché algérien des véhicules utilitaires”. “Répondre avec encore plus de flexibilité aux exigences locales” La marque MAN conservera sa position de “leader historique en tant que premier constructeur européen de véhicules utilitaires à importer des kits CKD en Algérie et à les assembler localement”. “En combinant notre expertise mondiale à de solides partenariats locaux, nous rapprochons nos produits de nos clients et répondons avec encore plus de flexibilité aux exigences locales”, a déclaré Friedrich Baumann, membre du directoire, en charge des ventes et des solutions client chez le constructeur allemand. “Forts de plus de 20 ans de collaboration fructueuse, nous sommes bien placés pour servir le marché algérien avec des véhicules de haute qualité, assemblés localement et adaptés aux besoins de nos clients”, a indiqué pour sa part Nabil Salhi, PDG de MTC. Le modèle CKD, ou Completely Knocked-Down, est “un élément central de la stratégie de croissance internationale de MAN Truck & Bus”, explique le constructeur. Il permet d'expédier des véhicules vers les marchés cibles sous forme de pièces détachées et assemblés localement, offrant ainsi des avantages économiques et réglementaires tout en facilitant l'accès au marché à long terme. L’article Automobile : l’allemand MAN reprend le montage en Algérie est apparu en premier sur TSA .
